Name: Is there a distinct category of dual for verbal agreement with a dual subject?
Feature_Description:
Dual is a grammatical number category that refers to two entities. This question asks whether there is agreement, i.e., that in addition to being märked on the subject, dual is also marked on the verb, as in the South Saami example (1). (1) South Saami
>*Månnoeh luhkien*
>we.1DU read.1DU
>‘We two read' Agreement may be consistent for various kinds of nouns (e.g., animate and inanimate nouns) and pronouns. In South Saami, there is dual agreement only for pronouns. In other instances, the plural form is used (cf. examples 2-3).
(2) South Saami
>*Maanah låhkijägan*
>child.PL read.3DU
>‘Children (= two) read' (3) South Saami
>*Göökte gaahtoeh byöpmedieh*
>two cat.PL eat.3PL
>‘Two cats eat' ## Coding The value is ‘1’ if there is verbal agreement with a dual subject in at least one of the possibilities mentioned above. The answer is ‘0’ if dual is marked only on the verb or only on the noun.

Name: Is non-agreement in number between the nominal subject and verbal predicate possible?
Feature_Description:
In addition to instances of agreement between the nominal subject and verbal predicate, there may also be examples of non-agreement, as in example (1). (1) Finnish
>*Lumeen jäi tasaiset jäljet*
>lume-en jä-i tasaise-t jälje-t
>snow-ILL remain-PST.3SG even-PL step-PL
>‘In the snow, there remained even steps’ It should be noted that this question only targets instances of formal agreement. When answering this question we did not consider instances where the subject is a quantity expression (2) or consists of coordinated items (3). (2) Estonian
>*Viis last läks ~ läksid poodi*
>five child.PRT go.PST.3SG ~ go.PST.3PL shop.ILL
>‘Five children went to the shop’ (3) Mari ([Alhoniemi 1985](sources.bib?label=Alhoniemi+1985#cldf:alhoniemi_marin_1985):73)
>*Otǝ̑štǝ̑ tumo, piste, pükšerme kuškǝ̑t*
>grove.INE oak linden nut.tree grow.3PL
>‘In the grove, there grow oak, linden, and nut trees’
## Coding The value is '1' if formal non-agreement can be attested. In other cases, incl. instances of semantic non-agreement, the value is '0'.

Name: Can finite verbs agree in number with a nominal object?
Feature_Description:
In addition to marking the P argument on the verb, a language may also encode the number of the object, as in Tundra Nenets (1a-b). This question maps languages, where such agreement is possible. (1) Tundra Nenets
>a. *xadaəda*
>xadaə-da
>kill-3SG>SG.OBJ
>‘He killed him/her’
>b. *xadaŋaxəyuda*
>xadaŋa-xəyu-da
>kill-DU.OBJ-3SG
>‘He killed them two’
([Nikolaeva 2014](sources.bib?label=Nikolaeva+2014#cldf:nikolaeva_grammar_2014):80) By comparison, in Hungarian, irrespective of the number of the object, the 1Sg personal ending in the definite conjugation is *-m* (2). Thus, Hungarian is coded as '0'. >(2) Hungarian
> *Elolvastam a könyvet / a könyveket*
> el-olvas-t-am a könyv-et / a könyv-ek-et*
>PFX-read-PST-1SG.DEF DEF.ART book-ACC / DEF.ART book-PL-ACC
>'I read the book / the books' ## Coding The value is ‘1’ if there is a way to mark the number of the object on the main verb (as in Tundra Nenets). If no such distinction can be made, the value is '0' (as in Hungarian).

Name: Can an adnominal property word agree with the noun in case?
Feature_Description:
This question asks whether the adnominal property word can take the same case form as the head noun (see example 1). Property words roughly correspond to adjectives. (1) Estonian
>*vanas majas*
>vana-s maja-s
>old-INE house-INE
>‘in an old house’ ## Coding The value is '1' if agreement is possible in at least some parts of the case paradigm, if not, the value is '0' (see also UT005). Adjectivized participial forms were not considered when answering this question.

Name: Is there non-agreement between an adnominal property word and the head noun?
Feature_Description:
Adjectives used as adnominal modifiers may share some but not necessarily all categories with their head nouns. For instance, in Veps, a comitative head noun is preceded by an adnominal property word in its genitive form (example 1). While Veps shows non-agreement only in some parts of the paradigm, there are languages like Mari (see example 2) that do not show any agreement between a modifier and the head noun at all. (1) Veps
>*penen mužikanke*
>pene-n mužika-nke
>short-GEN man-COM
>‘with a short man’
([Grünthal 2015](sources.bib?label=Gr%C3%BCnthal+2015#cldf:grunthal_vepsan_2015):16) (2) Meadow Mari
>*сылне сӱретлан*
>сылне сӱрет-лан
>beautiful picture-DAT
>'to the beautiful picture'
([Riese et al. 2019](sources.bib?label=Riese+et+al.+2019#cldf:riese_mari_2019)) ## Coding The value is '1' in both cases, i.e., when non-agreement is attested only in some parts of the paradigm as well as when there is no agreement within noun phrases at all. The value is '0' if agreement across the paradigm is obligatory.

Name: Can adpositions be used to mark core verbal arguments?
Feature_Description:
Subjects and objects are usually necessary to complete the meaning of a verb, which is why they are called *core arguments*. In languages that have cases, the distinction between subject and object is typically expressed by case marking. The aim of this question is to determine whether adpositions can also be used to mark them. For example, in the Mordvin languages there are four cases used for object marking: the nominative, genitive, inessive, and partitive (less common). As regards the inessive object, in Erzya, both the synthetic case suffix and an analytic postpositional construction can be used, while in Moksha, only the postpositional case form is available ([Hamari and Ajanki 2022](sources.bib?label=Hamari+and+Ajanki+2022#cldf:bakro-nagy_mordvin_2022):426-428; [Alhoniemi 1991](sources.bib?label=Alhoniemi+1991#cldf:alhoniemi_zur_1991):27). (1) Moksha
>*Лоткама васттнень эса ломаттне учихть **троллейбусть эса***
>lotkɑ-mɑ vɑst-tnǝn e-sɑ lomɑttnæ u͡tɕ-iȷ̊tj troljljejbus-tj e-sɑ
>stop-PTCP place-GEN.DEF.PL POSTP-INE person.NOM.DEF.PL wait-PRS.3PL trolleybus-GEN.DEF POSTP-INE
>‘At the stops, people are waiting for the trolleybus’
([Hamari and Ajanki 2022](sources.bib?label=Hamari+and+Ajanki+2022#cldf:bakro-nagy_mordvin_2022):427) ## Coding The value is '1' if it is possible to use an adpositional construction to mark at least one of the core arguments (S, A, and/or P) and the corresponding usage is not an instance of government, i.e., where a particular verb requires a certain adpositonal construction. When answering the question, passive clauses are not considered.

Name: Can non-finite clauses use a special means for core argument (S/A) marking?
Feature_Description:
Non-finite clauses can differ from finite clauses in terms of core argument marking. For instance, in Meadow Mari, the genitive case can be used to mark S/A in non-finite clauses: (1) Meadow Mari
>***унавлакын** кайымекышт, ме малаш возынна*
>unɑ-vlɑk-ən kɑjə-mekə-ʃt, me mɑl-ɑʃ voz-ən-nɑ
>guest-PL-GEN go-CVB.PRI-PX3PL 1PL sleep-INF lie.down-PST2-1PL
>'After the guests left, we went to sleep'
([Riese et al. 2022](sources.bib?label=Riese+et+al.+2022#cldf:riese_mari_2022):269) ## Coding The value is '1' if the S/A argument of the non-finite clause is encoded with a case that is not commonly used to encode the corresponding argument in the finite clause. For instance, in Mari, while the S/A argument of the non-finite clause may be encoded with the genitive case, in finite clauses the nominative case is typically used.

Name: Can non-finite clauses use a special means for core argument (P) marking?
Feature_Description:
Non-finite clauses can differ from finite clauses in terms of core argument marking. This question asks whether P in non-finite clauses can be marked in a way that is not typical for finite clauses. For instance, in Hill Mari, the nominative can encode objects in non-finite clauses (1-2), while this is not common for finite clauses ([Saarinen 2022](sources.bib?label=Saarinen+2022#cldf:bakro-nagy_mari_2022):438-439). (1) Hill Mari
>*Салтак йӓрӹшкӹ **кол** кычаш ашкедӹм*
>Sɑltɑk jær-ə̈ʃkə̈ kol kət͡ʃ-ɑʃ ɑʃked-ə̈m
>Saltak lake-ILL fish.NOM catch-INF go-PST1.1SG
>'I walked to Lake Saltak to go fishing'
([Krasnova et al. 2017](sources.bib?label=Krasnova+et+al.+2017#cldf:krasnova_reading_2017):292) (2) Meadow Mari
>***паша** ыштышыла*
>pɑʃɑ əʃtə-ʃəlɑ
>work.NOM do-CVB
>‘while working’
([Saarinen 2022](sources.bib?label=Saarinen+2022#cldf:bakro-nagy_mari_2022):438) For a special means of S/A marking in non-finite clauses, see UT008. ## Coding The value is '1' if the P argument of the non-finite clause is encoded in a way that is not commonly used to encode the corresponding argument in the finite clause.

Name: Do the characteristics of the object referent (topicality, animacy, definiteness) influence object marking?
Feature_Description:
In languages with overt case-marking of direct objects, the marking may depend on semantic and pragmatic features; a phenomenon known as differential object marking (DOM) ([Aissen 2003](sources.bib?label=Aissen+2003#cldf:Aissen2003)). This question asks whether differences in object marking are conditioned by certain characteristics of an object, such as topicality, animacy, or definiteness. For example, in Komi, definite inanimate objects can be either marked or unmarked (1a), whereas animate objects obligatorily take the accusative case (1b). (1) Komi Zyrian
>a. *Ме любита **Сыктывкарöс ~ Сыктывкар***
>me ʎubit-ɑ Sɨktɨvkɑr-ɘs ~ Sɨktɨvkɑr
>1SG love-PRS.1SG Syktyvkar-ACC ~ Syktyvkar[ACC]
>‘I like Syktyvkar’ >b. *Ме любита **Бобикöс***
>me ʎubit-ɑ Bobik-ɘs
>1SG love-PRS.1SG Bobik-ACC
>‘I like Bobik'
([Klumpp 2012](sources.bib?label=Klumpp+2012#cldf:Klumpp_2012):345) Cf. the prominecy scale ([Aissen 2003](sources.bib?label=Aissen+2003#cldf:Aissen2003)): - Animacy scale: HUMAN > ANIMATE > INANIMATE - Definiteness scale: PERSONAL PRONOUN > PROPER NAME > DEFINITE NP > INDEFINITE SPECIFIC NP > NON-SPECIFIC NP ## Coding The value is '1' if at least one of the above mentioned characteristics of an object referent causes differences in direct object marking. When answering the question, only finite clauses are considered.

Name: Does object marking correlate with aspectuality?
Feature_Description:
Although differential object marking (DOM) is generally based on hierarchies reflecting certain characteristics of an object (such as animacy, topicality, definiteness; see UT010), there are also languages that use DOM to convey aspectual nuances, e.g., perfectivity vs. imperfectivity ([Malchukov and de Hoop 2011](sources.bib?label=Malchukov+and+de+Hoop+2011#cldf:Malchukov2011)). In Estonian, the genitive object tends to receive a perfective/bounded interpretation (1a), whereas the partitive object gets an imperfcetive/unbounded interpretation (1b). (1) Estonian
>a. *Anne ostis **jalgratta***
>Anne ost-is jalgratta
>Anne buy-PST.3SG bicycle.GEN
>‘Anne bought a bicycle’ >b. *Anne ostis **jalgratast***
>Anne ost-is jalgratas-t
>Anne buy-PST.3SG bicycle-PRT
>‘Anne was buying a bicycle’
## Coding The value is '1' if differences in direct object marking are based on differences in conveying aspectual information. The answer is '1' even if there are some restrictions, e.g., in Erzya, the definite conjugation can be associated with perfectivity/future and the indefinite conjugation with imperfectivity/present, although this does not apply to verbs expressing state and perception. When answering the question, only finite clauses were considered.

Name: Does object marking depend on the verb conjugation form?
Feature_Description:
The marking of the direct object may depend on the verb form (see also UT010 and UT011). For instance, in Estonian, the imperative verb form takes an object in the nominative case (1a) and the negative verb form requires an object in the partitive case (1b). (1) Estonian
>a. *Võta **võti**!*
> take.IMP.2SG key.NOM
> ‘Take the key’ >b. *Ära võta **võtit**!*
> ära võta võti-t
> NEG.IMP.2SG take.CNG key-PRT
> ‘Don’t take the key!’ ## Coding The value is '1' if the verb conjugation form can have an effect on the case selection of the object (as in Estonian). When answering the question, only finite forms were considered; object conjugation was not considered.

Name: Is there differential subject marking?
Feature_Description:
In addition to differential object marking (see UT010-UT012), languages may also show differential subject marking (DSM). In a broad sense, a different case for a subject, or a different agreement or position for a subject can already count as DSM; in a narrower sense, such differences ought to depend on the features of the subject in some way to be regarded as DSM ([Woolford 2009](sources.bib?label=Woolford+2009#cldf:Woolford2009):17). Here, we are only interested in differences in case marking in finite clauses that depend on the features of the subject. For example, in Estonian, the nominative case may be used for identification or expressing a definite amount of something (1a), whereas the partitive case may be used to express an indefinite amount of something (1b). The Estonian examples are both instances of an existential clause. (1) Estonian
>a. *Laual on **leib***
>laua-l on leib
>table-ADE be.3SG bread.NOM
>‘There is the bread on the table’ >b. *Laual on **leiba***
>laua-l on leiba
>table-ADE be.3SG bread.PRT
>‘There is some bread on the table’ ## Coding The value is '1', if subject marking differs depending on the features of the subject. When answering the question, only finite clauses were considered.

Name: Can S and A be conflated morphologically across clause boundaries, i.e., act as a syntactic pivot?
Feature_Description:
"If two clauses within a complex sentence have an argument in common, a syntactic pivot may determine which co-referetial participants within a sentence can be ellipsed, and which not" ([Aikhenvald 2015](sources.bib?label=Aikhenvald+2015#cldf:aikhenvald_2015):257). If S (= subject in an intransitive sentence) and A (=subject in a transitive sentence) can be conflated, the language has an 'accusative' pivot (cf. UT015 for ergative pivot). This is true for Estonian (1), where Ø can only be understood to refer to the subject (i.e., it was the thief and not the woman who ran away). (1) Estonian
>***Varas** (A) lõi naist (P) ja Ø (S) jooksis ära*
>varas lõ-i nais-t ja jooks-is ära
>thief hit-PST.3SG woman-PRT and run-PST.3SG away
>‘The thief hit the woman and ran away’ ## Coding The value is '1' if the shared argument that is omitted functions as the subject.

Name: Can S and P be conflated morphologically across clause boundaries, i.e., act as a syntactic pivot?
Feature_Description:
Apart from languages where S and A can be conflated across clause boundaries (cf. UT014) there are also languages that can combine S (= subject in an intransitive sentence) and P (= object in a transitive sentence), which implies an 'ergative' pivot (see [Aikhenvald 2015](sources.bib?label=Aikhenvald+2015#cldf:aikhenvald_2015):258). East Mansi (1) shows this possibility. (1) East Mansi
>***tolmax** (A) neem (P) senktǝstǝ, Ø il xajtǝs*
>tolmax nee-m senktǝ-stǝ, il xajtǝs
>thief woman-ACC hit-PST.SG>3SG away run-PST.3SG
>'The thief hit the woman, and she ran away' ## Coding The value is '1' if S and P can be conflated (as in East Mansi). The value is '0' if the shared argument that is omitted functions as S or A (cf. UT014).

Name: Is there grammatical marking of habituality?
Feature_Description:
The habitual expresses a situation (an event or state of affairs)that can be viewed as characteristic of an extended period of time ([Comrie 1976](sources.bib?label=Comrie+1976#cldf:comrie_1976): 27-28). This question asks whether a language has means other than lexical ones to express habituality. In Tundra Nenets, there is an morphological marker for conveying habituality (1), whereas in Meadow Mari a periphrastic device (a compound past tense) is used (2). (1) Tunda Nenets
>*mənc°ras´ətid°m*
>mənc°ra-s´əti-d°m
>work-HAB-1SG
>‘I usually work’
>([Nikolaeva 2014](sources.bib?label=Nikolaeva+2014#cldf:nikolaeva_grammar_2014):84) (2) Meadow Mari
>*Йыван ончыч тамак-ым **ок шупш ыле***
>jəvɑn ont͡ɕət͡ɕ tɑmɑk-əm ok ʃupʃ əʎe
>Yyvan before tobacco-ACC NEG.3SG smoke.CNG be.PST1.3SG
>'Yyvan didn’t use to smoke'
>([Riese et al. 2019](sources.bib?label=Riese+et+al.+2019#cldf:riese_mari_2019):224)
## Coding The value is '1' if the expression of habituality can be clearly attributed to a grammatical device.

Name: Is there grammatical marking of frequentativity?
Feature_Description:
The frequentative is used to describe a situation (an event or state of affairs) as frequently repeating. The aim of this question is to determine whether frequentativity can be expressed by means other than lexical ones. Both in North Mansi (1) and Hungarian (2), a derivational suffix is used to convey frequentativity. (1) North Mansi
>*suns-* vs. *suns-**iyl**-*
>'to watch' vs. 'to watch repeatedly'
>([Riese 2001](sources.bib?label=Riese+2001#cldf:riese_vogul_2001):58) (2) Hungarian
>*olvas* vs. *olvas-**gat***
>'to read' vs. 'to read repeatedly' ## Coding The value is '1' if the expression of frequentativity can be clearly attributed to a grammatical device. If only lexical means are available in a language, the answer is '0'.

Name: Is there grammatical marking of progressivity?
Feature_Description:
The progressive views the situation “as ongoing at a single point in time, but it does not exhaustively localize the event” ([Bertinetto et al. 2000](sources.bib?label=Bertinetto+et+al.+2000#cldf:bertinetto_et_al_2000):527). Historically, progressives often have locative sources, and development into progressives has involved erosion of the locative meaning (e.g., B[ybee et al. 1994](sources.bib?label=ybee+et+al.+1994#cldf:bybee_et_al_1994)). This question asks whether a language contains a dedicated means to express progressivity. Finnish and South Saami use a periphrastic construction consisting of an auxiliary 'be' + a non-finite verb form (1-2). In South Saami, the auxiliary verb may be dropped. (1) Finnish
>***Olen kirjoittamassa** pientä artikkelia*
>ole-n kirjoitta-ma-ssa pien-tä artikkelia
>be-1SG read-SUP-INE snall-PRT article.PRT
>‘I am writing a small article’ (2) South Saami
>*Manne Staaresne **årroeminie***
>1SG Staare.INE live.PROG
>‘I am living in Östersund’ ## Coding The value is '1' if there is a grammatical device that can be associated with the expression of progressivity. The value is '1' even if in some contexts the corresponding device may appear with a non-progressive (such as locative) meaning.

Name: Is it common to use a verb in the present tense for future time reference?
Feature_Description:
Future time reference (FTR) may be expressed in several ways. One option is to use the present tense form of the lexical verb and deduce the future meaning from the context, e.g., a temporal adverb (such as 'tomorrow', 'next year') may give rise to a future reading; aspectual relations may also play a role ([Dahl 2000](sources.bib?label=Dahl+2000#cldf:Dahl_2000)). Usage of the present tense for FTR is common, for instance, in Hungarian (1). (1) Hungarian
>*Jövő télen **veszek** egy új autót*
>jövő tél-en vesz-ek egy új autó-t
>next winter-SUPESS buy-PRS.1SG a new car-ACC
>‘Next winter I will buy a new car’
>([Rounds 2001](sources.bib?label=Rounds+2001#cldf:rounds_hungarian_2001):50) ## Coding The value is '1' if FTR is commonly expressed using the verb in its present tense form. If a language has a dedicated future marker (an inflection, auxiliary, or derivation), the answer is usually '0'.

Name: Is there grammatical marking of the immediate future?
Feature_Description:
A language may contain more than one means for expressing future time reference. For instance, there may be a separate device for expressing the immediate future (B[ybee et al. 1994](sources.bib?label=ybee+et+al.+1994#cldf:bybee_et_al_1994)). For the immediate future, a situation can be viewed as about to take place. Whereas in Nganasan, the resultative has this function (1), in Estonian, a periphrastic device may be used (2). (1) Nganasan
>*tǝuɁkiɁinǝ*
>tǝu-Ɂki-Ɂi-nǝ
>fly-RES-AOR.RFL-1SG.RFL
>'I'm just flying to Dudinka'
>([Wagner-Nagy 2018](sources.bib?label=Wagner-Nagy+2018#cldf:wagner-nagy_grammar_2018):212) (2) Estonian
>*Rong **on väljumas***
>rong on välju-ma-s
>train be.3SG leave-SUP-INE
>'The train is about to leave' ## Coding The value is '1' if there is a grammatical device (either morphological or periphrastic), which can be used to express an action that is about to happen (regardless of other possible functions the corresponding device may have). Instances of a lexical verb used in the past tense form to convey an immediate activity were not considered (e.g., Estonian *Läksime!* go.PST.1PL ‘Off we go!’, cf. Russian *пошли*).

Name: Is there grammatical marking of the pluperfect?
Feature_Description:
The pluperfect is used to locate a situation in the past prior to a reference point that is also in the past; thus, it can be regarded as 'past in the past' ([Comrie 1985](sources.bib?label=Comrie+1985#cldf:comrie_1985):65). Whereas in example (1) the referece point is established by the subordinate clause, example (2) shows that the reference time is not necessarily expressed in the same sentence. Both periphrastic (1) as well as morphological means (2) suffice for '1'. (1) Estonian
>*Kui ma saabusin, **oli** ta juba **lahkunud***
>kui ma saabu-si-n, ol-i ta juba lahku-nud
>when 1SG arrive-PST-1SG be-PST.3SG 3SG already leave-ACT.PST.PTCP
>‘When I arrived, s/he had already left’ (2) Nganasan
>*sɨtɨ bǝðürbüǝ ńemɨnǝ sǝd'ǝǝ-mǝnu **kontud'üǝd'ǝǝ***
>sɨtɨ bǝðürbüǝ ńemɨ-nǝ sǝd'ǝǝ-mǝnu kontu-d'üǝd'ǝǝ
>s/he dead.man.[ACC] mother-GEN.1SGPOSS way-PROL carry-PPF.[3SG]
>'He had carried a dead body across my mother's way'
>([Wagner-Nagy 2018](sources.bib?label=Wagner-Nagy+2018#cldf:wagner-nagy_grammar_2018)) ## Coding The value is '1' if there is a dedicated pluperfect device, either periphrastic or morphological.

Name: Can aspect be expressed overtly in the negative marker?
Feature_Description:
Languages that allow grammatical categories to occur in the negative marker (typically called the *negative auxiliary*) differ as to which grammatical categories can be expressed in the negative auxiliary and which in the lexical verb. This question investigates whether aspect can occur in the negative marker. For instance, in Nganasan, the iterative suffix *-kǝ* and habitual suffix *-muŋha* (both derivational) appear in the negative auxiliary ([Gusev 2015](sources.bib?label=Gusev+2015#cldf:gusev_2015):107). (1) Nganasan
>*munugǝtu* vs. ***ńigǝti̮** munǝˀ*
>munu-gǝ-tu vs. ńi-gǝ-ti̮ munǝ-ˀ
>say-ITER-PRS.3SG vs. NEG-ITER-PRS.3SG say-CNG
>'(S)he regularly says' vs. '(S)he doesn't (regularly) say'
([Gusev 2015](sources.bib?label=Gusev+2015#cldf:gusev_2015):107) ## Coding The value is '1' if aspect can be expressed overtly in the negative marker. The value is '0' if such marking is not possible.

Name: Can tense be expressed overtly in the negative marker?
Feature_Description:
Languages that allow grammatical categories to occur in the negative marker (usually called the *negative auxiliary*) differ as to which grammatical categories can be expressed in the negative auxiliary and which in the lexical verb (cf. also UT022). The aim of this question is to determine whether tense can be expressed in the negative marker, as in South Saami (cf. 1a and 1b). (1) South Saami
>a. ***Im** lohkh*
>NEG.1SG read.CNG
>‘I do not read’ >b. ***Idtjim** lohkh*
>NEG.PST.1SG read.CNG
>‘I did not read’ ## Coding The value is '1' if tense can be overtly expressed in the negative marker. If no such marking is possible, the value is '0'.

Name: Is there grammatical marking of mirativity?
Feature_Description:
Mirativity is a grammatical category used to denote new, unexpected, and/or surprising situations. Although mirativity may be viewed as a subcategory of evidentiality (inferentiality) since it often arises as an extension of evidentiality, there are also reasons to view it separately: (i) an unexpected situation is not necessarily inferred; (ii) it may emerge as an extension of tense or mood [](sources.bib?label=#cldf:oudb_ob-ugric_2017)([OUDB)](sources.bib?label=OUDB%29#cldf:oudb_ob-ugric_2017). Mirativity is regarded as a separate category in North Mansi (example 1), where it is shown to have evolved through desubordination (see [Skribnik 2005](sources.bib?label=Skribnik+2005#cldf:skribnik_complex_2005)). In Khanty dialects, the categories of evidentiality and mirativity reveal different origins ([Nikolaeva 1999](sources.bib?label=Nikolaeva+1999#cldf:nikolaeva_semantics_1999)). (1) North Mansi
>*Anʲ aːm jujipaːlumt taj maːkoləŋ oːjka ta **joːmiɣt-aneːte***
>Anʲ aːm jujipaːl-um-t taj maːkoləŋ oːjka ta joːmiɣt-aneː-te
>now I behind-SG<1SG-LOC EMPH bear ACT1 lunge.out-MIR.PRS-3SG
>‘There is a bear lunging out behind meǃ’
>[](sources.bib?label=#cldf:oudb_ob-ugric_2017)([OUDB)](sources.bib?label=OUDB%29#cldf:oudb_ob-ugric_2017) ## Coding The value is '1' if there is a dedicated marker to denote new, unexpected, and/or surprising situations. Both morphological and periphrastic means count.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of a desire or wish?
Feature_Description:
Desiderative modality expresses a desire or wish of the subject referent to bring about a particular state of affairs. For example, Mari has a separate desiderative mood marked with *-ne* (1); it has no verb meaning 'to want' ([Riese et al. 2019](sources.bib?label=Riese+et+al.+2019#cldf:riese_mari_2019):238-246). (The term *optative* may also be used.) (1) Meadow Mari
>*луднем : луднет* etc.
>lud-ne-m : lud-ne-t etc.
>read-DES-1SG : read-DES-2SG
>‘I, you etc. want to/intend to read'
([Riese et al. 2019](sources.bib?label=Riese+et+al.+2019#cldf:riese_mari_2019):238) ## Coding The value is '1' if there is a dedicated marker for the expression of a desire or wish and the marker is available with at least some lexical verbs and it occurs in different persons. Examples of a conditional mood used in a similar meaning do not suffice for '1', since the situation is regarded as possible only under certain conditions.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to irrealis?
Feature_Description:
It is possible to distinguish between events that have actualised (*realis*) and events that can only be imagined or wished for (*irrealis*) ([Mithun 1995](sources.bib?label=Mithun+1995#cldf:Mithun1995)). This question asks whether morphological marking is used to encode events regarded as nonactualised; see the conditional marking used in example (1). (Various terms may be used, e.g., *conditional*, *conjunctive*, *subjunctive*.) (1) Estonian
>*Ma **elaksin** maal, kui **saaksin***
>Ma ela-ksi-n maa-l, kui saa-ksi-n
>1SG live-COND-1SG country_side-ADE if can-COND-1SG
>'I would live in the countryside if I could' ## Coding The value is '1' if morphological marking is used to denote events regarded as nonactualised. In order to suffice for ‘1’ morphological marking has to be available at least for some lexical verbs. Thus, instances where irrealis is only expressed by phonologically independent means (such as particles or auxiliaries) count as '0'.

Name: Are verbs marked with a non-indicative mood in counterfactual conditional clauses?
Feature_Description:
Counterfactuality may be defined as the “fictitious creation of a possible world which does not correspond to the actual world and therefore is “contrary to fact”" ([Squartini 2016](sources.bib?label=Squartini+2016#cldf:squartini_2016):64). Counterfactuality may be expressed in connection with past, present, and future. In all cases, the protasis is contrary to fact, false, or unlikely. This question investigates whether the verb of the protasis is marked with a specific mood (the terms *conditional*, *irrealis* may be used). For example, Finnish (1) and Nganasan (2) make use of conditional marking. (1) Finnish
>*Jos **olisit** leiponut kakun, söisin sen*
>jos ol-isi-t leipo-nut kaku-n, sö-isi-n se-n
>if be-COND-2SG bake-ACT.PST.PTCP cake-GEN eat-COND-1SG it-GEN
>’If you had baked a cake, I would eat it’ (2) Nganasan
>*mɨŋ ŋəməɁkəsuə **ihünüɁ** ńaagəiɁ kuntubaaðəińüɁ*
>mɨŋ ŋəmə-Ɂkə-suə i-hü-nüɁ ńaagəi-Ɂ kuntu-baaðəi-ńüɁ
>we eat-RES-PST.[3SG] be-COND-OBL.1PLPOSS good-ADV sleep-IRR.RFL-1PL.RFL
>‘If we had eaten, we would have slept well’
([Wagner-Nagy 2018](sources.bib?label=Wagner-Nagy+2018#cldf:wagner-nagy_grammar_2018)) ## Coding The value is '1' if in counterfactual conditional clauses verbs of the protasis (typically the 'if'-clause) are marked with a non-indicative mood.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of deontic necessity?
Feature_Description:
Deontic necessity can be associated with the expression of obligation, the source of which is the speaker or another source (some authority). In Tundra Nenets, the debitive is one of several moods used to express obligation (1). Here, we are not interested in verbs marked with the imperative mood. (1) Tundra Nenets
>*mənʹ° sʹit° **xadawəntad°m***
>mənʹ° sʹit° xada-wənta-d°m
>I you.ACC kill-DEB-1SG
>‘I must kill you’
([Nikolaeva 2014](sources.bib?label=Nikolaeva+2014#cldf:nikolaeva_grammar_2014):195) ## Coding The value is '1' if there is a dedicated morphological marker used to express deontic necessity. The answer is '0' if necessity can only be conveyed using lexical means, analytical constructions, or particles.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of epistemic possibility?
Feature_Description:
Epistemic modality can be defined as the “speaker’s evaluation of the likelihood of a state of affairs” ([Nuyts 2001](sources.bib?label=Nuyts+2001#cldf:Nuyts2001):xv). For instance, *Jane is probably/may be at work* can be analysed as follows: the speaker holds that the proposition "Jane is at work" is not certain but only a mere possibility, relative to what s/he knows or his/her evidence. This question asks whether there is morphological marking available in such instances (see example 1). (1) Finnish
>*Porukan vanhimpana hän **puhunee** kokemuksesta*
>poruka-n vanh-i-mpa-na hän puhu-nee kokemukse-sta
>group-GEN old-PL-CMPR-ESS 3SG speak-POT.3SG experience-ELA
>‘As the oldest in the group, he probably speaks from experience’ ## Coding The value is '1' if epistemic possibility can be expressed using a dedicated morphological means. If a language only contains lexical means or phonologically independent means (particles, auxiliaries), the value is '0'.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of deontic possibility?
Feature_Description:
Deontic possibility has to do with permission, e.g., *You may leave the hospital after a week*. This question asks whether there is a morphological marker, which can be used to convey such a meaning. In Hungarian, the suffix *-het/-hat* is used to give permission (1) (additionally it functions as a marker of epistemic possibility). (1) Hungarian
>*bejöhetsz*
>bejö-het-sz
>in_come-POT-2SG
>‘you may come in’ ## Coding The answer is '1' if there is a dedicated morphological marker used to grant permission. If a language only makes use of lexical means or phonologically independent means (particles, auxiliaries), the value is '0'.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb used to direct commands/wishes, etc. to the 3Sg?
Feature_Description:
A command addressed to a singular 2nd person can be regarded as a canonical imperative, but commands can also be addressed to other persons ([Aikhenvald 2015](sources.bib?label=Aikhenvald+2015#cldf:aikhenvald_2015): 234). The aim of this question is to determine whether a non-indicative morphological marking can be used on the verb to direct a command/wish, etc. to the singular 3rd person, as in (1) (cf. also UT032). (The corresponding form is usually called a *jussive*, *optative*, or *imperative*.) (1) Finnish
>*Hän **tulkoon** sisään*
>hän tul-koon sisään
>s/he come-JUSS.3SG inside
>‘Let him/her come inside!’ ## Coding The value is '1' if a morphological marking (different from the indicative) can appear on the verb to convey a command directed to the singular 3rd person. If there is no such marking or this function is encoded only by a particle the value is '0'.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb used to direct commands/wishes, etc. to the 1Sg?
Feature_Description:
In addition to commands addressed to a singular 2nd person it is also possible to direct commands to other persons (see also UT031). This question asks whether a non-indicative morphological marking can be used on the verb to direct a command/wish, etc. to the singular 1st person, as in example (1). (The corresponding form is usually called a *jussive*, *optative*, *imperative* or *hortative*.) (1) Estonian
>*Ta soovitas, et ma **mingu** ees*
>Ta soovita-s, et ma min-gu ees
>3SG suggest-PST.3SG that 1SG go-JUSS in_front
>‘S/he suggested that I should go ahead’ ## Coding The value is '1' if a morphological marking can be used on the verb to direct a command/wish, etc. to a singular 1st person. If there is no such marking or this function is encoded only by a particle the value is '0'.

Name: Is it common to use the imperative/jussive in complement clauses introduced by predicates expressing wish/desire/will?
Feature_Description:
Complement clauses introduced by verbs of different semantic types (e.g., 'want', 'order', 'see' etc.) may require a specific marking on the verb in the complement clause. The aim of this question is to find out whether imperative/jussive marking occurs in complement clauses introduced by verbs conveying 'wish', 'want', or related meanings (see example 1). (Instead of *imperative* or *jussive* the terms *subjunctive* or *conjunctive* may also be used.) (1) Hungarian
>*Nem akar-ja, hogy **kijöjjünk** a víz-ből*
>NEG want-3SG.DEF that out_come.SUBJ.1PL.INDEF DEF.ART water-ELA
>'S/he doesn't want us to come out of the water'
>([Rounds 2001](sources.bib?label=Rounds+2001#cldf:rounds_hungarian_2001)) ## Coding The value is '1' if imperative/jussive marking can appear on the verb in the complement clause introduced by predicates expressing wish, desire, or will.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of apprehension?
Feature_Description:
The apprehensive marker is used to express fear or regret about a certain situation; the state of affairs may be viewed as something undesirable. In Tundra Nenets, the apprehensive mood is used to convey fear about the future, in which case the apprehensive marker typically occurs on the negative auxiliary (see 1); a further meaning is regret about the past (see 2) ([Nikolaeva 2014](sources.bib?label=Nikolaeva+2014#cldf:nikolaeva_grammar_2014):90-91). (1) Tundra Nenets
>*yer°mpoy° nʹū-mʹi ŋanʹih nʹi-r°wa-q xaŋkul°-q!*
>INTRJ child-1SG again NEG-APPR-REFL.3SG get.ill-CONNEG
>‘If only my child wouldn’t get ill!’
([Nikolaeva 2014](sources.bib?label=Nikolaeva+2014#cldf:nikolaeva_grammar_2014):90) (2) Tundra Nenets
>*nʹer°q tū-l°wa-n°=wa*
>earlier come-APPR-2SG-ASS
>‘If only you had come earlier!’
([Nikolaeva 2014](sources.bib?label=Nikolaeva+2014#cldf:nikolaeva_grammar_2014):90) ## Coding The answer is '1' if there is a dedicated morphological marker used to express apprehension. If a language only makes use of lexical means or phonologically independent means (particles, auxiliaries), the value is '0'.

Name: Is there a reflexive conjugation?
Feature_Description:
In the case of a reflexive verb, an agent can be viewed as performing an action directed at itself; such verbs are often accompanied by reflexive pronouns (e.g., *dress oneself*). Here, we are interested to determine whether a language contains a separate paradigm of verb affixes used to convey reflexivity, as in Veps (cf. 1a and 1b). (1) Veps
>a. *pezen : pezed*, etc. (non-reflexive conjugation)
>peze-n : peze-d
>wash-1SG : wash-2SG
>‘I wash' : 'you wash’, etc.
>b. *pezemoi : pezetoi*, etc. (reflexive conjugation)
>peze-moi : peze-toi
>wash-REFL.1SG : wash-REFL.2SG
>‘I wash myself’ : ‘you wash yourself’, etc.
([Grünthal 2015](sources.bib?label=Gr%C3%BCnthal+2015#cldf:grunthal_vepsan_2015): 143) ## Coding The value is '1' if the reflexive conjugation is available in various persons and it differs from the non-reflexive conjugation (as in Veps). If a language only contains a reflexive derivational suffix, the value is '0'. For example, in Meadow Mari the derivational suffix *-alt* precedes the personal suffixes and it does not vary throughout the paradigm (Riese et al. 2022: 383). Thus, for Meadow Mari the value is '0'.

Name: Can non-singular 2nd and 3rd person have the same person/number marking or morphosyntactic treatment?
Feature_Description:
In the case of syncretism, a single form is used to express two or more functions within the inflectional paradigm. This question aims to capture syncretism in non-singular 2nd and 3rd person forms. In Livonian, for instance, there is syncretism in the past tense forms (1), whereas in Tundra Nenets syncretism involves the present tense forms of the objective conjugation in the dual (2). (1) Livonian
>*jelīztõ*
>jel-īz-tõ
>live-PST-2PL/3PL
>‘you/they lived’ (2) Tundra Nenets
>*meŋaxəyud′ih*
>meŋa-xəyu-d′ih
>take-DU.OBJ-2DU/3DU
>‘you two/they two take (sth)’
>([Nikolaeva 2014](sources.bib?label=Nikolaeva+2014#cldf:nikolaeva_grammar_2014):79) ## Coding The value is '1' if non-singular 2nd and 3rd person can be marked the same way, which makes them different (at least) from the non-singular 1st person.

Name: Can the 3rd person plural verb form and impersonal (passive) verb form be encoded in the same way?
Feature_Description:
This question asks whether one and the same verb form can appear in the finite paradigm (i.e., the 3rd person plural verb form) as well as impersonal (or passive) paradigm. For instance, this is the case in Livonian (1a-b) and Veps (2a-b). (1) Livonian
> a. *ne **vȯstīztõ** būomvillizt luoimõ*
>ne vȯst-īz-tõ būomvilliz-t luoimõ
> 3PL buy-PST-3PL cotton.ADJ-PRT thread.PRT
>‘They bought cotton thread’
>b. *Siz mūndakõrd **vȯstīztõ** būomvillizt luoimõ*
>siz mūndakõrd vȯst-īz-tõ būomvilliz-t luoimõ
>then sometimes buy-PST-3PL cotton.ADJ-PRT thread.PRT
>‘Then sometimes cotton thread was bought’
>([Viitso 2008](sources.bib?label=Viitso+2008#cldf:viitso_liivi_2008):321) (2) Veps
>a. *hijamot **tehtas** paidha, ombeldas*
>hijamo-t teh-tas paid-ha, ombel-das
> sleeve-PL make-PRS.PASS shirt-ILL sew-PRS.PASS
>‘Sleeves are made and sewn onto the shirt’
>([Grünthal 2015](sources.bib?label=Gr%C3%BCnthal+2015#cldf:grunthal_vepsan_2015):258) >b. *hö **tehtas***
> hö teh-tas
> 3PL make-PRS.PASS
> ‘They make’
## Coding The value is '1' if one and the same verb form can be used on both occasions.

Name: Are 2nd person singular verb forms commonly used with a generic meaning?
Feature_Description:
The canonical function of the 2nd person singular forms is to refer to the addressee. However, they may also receive a generic interpretation when used without any specific 2nd person addressee in mind. There are instances that are unambiguously generic as otherwise the interpretation would literally be wrong, while other sentences allow for several interpretations ([Gast et al. 2015](sources.bib?label=Gast+et+al.+2015#cldf:Gast2015):148-149). Although without a broader context the Veps example (1) could be directed to the addressee, 2Sg forms are also attested with a generic function (see [Grünthal 2015](sources.bib?label=Gr%C3%BCnthal+2015#cldf:grunthal_vepsan_2015):125). >(1) Veps
>*Oludespai **humalstud***
>olude-spai humalstu-d
>beer-ELA get_drunk-2SG
>'One gets drunk from beer’
([Grünthal 2015](sources.bib?label=Gr%C3%BCnthal+2015#cldf:grunthal_vepsan_2015): 125) ## Coding The value is '1' if 2Sg forms are commonly found to express a generic meaning in a language. If 2Sg forms are possible but rare, the value is '0'

Name: Can an infinitival verb form be used in a directive function?
Feature_Description:
This question asks whether an infinitival verb form can appear as the only predicate in the clause with a directive function (example 1). (1) North Saami
>*Ii **doarrut**!*
>*Ii doarru-t!*
>NEG.3SG quarrel-INF
>'Do not quarrel!'
The addressee of a directive expressed with an infinitive is an impersonal or implicit referent. Infinitive directives can have several specific functions in discourse: they are used, e.g., as generic instructions, strong commands, curt commands, proscriptions, instructions. ([Aikhenvald 2010](sources.bib?label=Aikhenvald+2010#cldf:aikhenvald_2010):281-283) ## Coding The value is '1' if an infinitive can be used in an independent clause (main clause) with a directive function. The value is '0' if there are no such examples or an infinitive only appears as a result of dropping an auxiliary. (Although infinitives together with participles and converbs can be regarded as non-finite forms, this question only targets infinitives.)

Name: Can verbs in purposive subordinate clauses be infinitive forms?
Feature_Description:
A subordinate (or dependent) clause functions as a constituent of the main clause. Purposive clauses are a type of subordinate clauses used to express meanings like ‘in order to’, ‘so that’ etc. (2). Purposive clauses function as adjuncts - typically of adverbial clauses - as they are not obligatory constituents of the clause. Here, we also considered purposive clauses introduced by a complementizer, such as *et* in example (1b). (The purposive clause may also be called a *final clause*.) (1) Estonian
>a. *Ma lähen Ungarisse ungari keelt **õppima***
>ma lähe-n Ungari-sse ungari keel-t õppi-ma
>1SG go-1SG Hungary-ILL Hungarian language-PRT study-INF2
>b. *Ma lähen Ungarisse, et ungari keelt **õppida***
>ma lähe-n Ungari-sse, et ungari keel-t õppi-da
>1SG go-1SG Hungary-ILL in_order Hungarian language-PRT study-INF1
>'I’m going to Hungary in order to study Hungarian'
## Coding The value is '1' if it is possible to use an infinitive form in the purposive subordinate clause (1a-b).

Name: Are there sentences without overt verbal arguments?
Feature_Description:
There may be verbs that do not require any verbal arguments (such as a subject or an object) but can form sentences on their own. This is often attested with the so-called meteorological or weather verbs, as in examples (1-2). (1) Finnish
>*Sataa*
>rain.3SG
>'It is raining' (2) South Saami
>*Tjåetskeme*
>cold_weather.NMLZ
>‘It is cold’ ## Coding The value is '1' if there are avalent verbs, i.e., verbs that do not need any arguments. The value is '0' if it is necessary to use a subject, including a dummy subject or expletive subject (such as *it* in the translations above).

Name: Is a copula commonly used for predicate nominals in the 3rd person singular forms of the present tense?
Feature_Description:
Clauses that express proper inclusion, equation, attribution, location, existence, and possession may be collectively referred to as predicate nominals. Here, we are only interested in what could, in a more restricted sense, be referred to as *predicate nominals* and *predicate adjectives*. The respective clauses typically express equation (*He is my father*), proper inclusion (*He is a teacher*), and attribution (*He is tall*). The term *copula* designates a verb (e.g., *be* in example 1 and 2), an affix, or a particle that joins two nominal elements and functions as the predicate nucleus ([Payne 1997](sources.bib?label=Payne+1997#cldf:payne_1997):115; [Pustet 2003](sources.bib?label=Pustet+2003#cldf:pustet_2003):5). The aim of this question is to determine whether predicate nominal and prediate adjective clauses typically follow the structure *NP copula NP* (see example 1) or *NP copula AdjP* (see example 2) when used for the 3rd person singular and present time reference. (1) Estonian
>*Ta **on** õpetaja*
>s/he be.3SG teacher
>'S/he is a teacher’ (2) Skolt Saami
>*Nijdd **lij** äʹrǧǧ*
>girl.SG.NOM PRS.3SG shy.SG.NOM
>'The girl is shy'
([Feist 2015](sources.bib?label=Feist+2015#cldf:feist_grammar_2015):179) ## Coding The value is '1' if a copula is needed/commonly used in the simplest predicate nominal constructions, i.e., the present tense. When answering this question, only instances of 3Sg and present affirmative forms were considered.

Name: Is a copula commonly used for predicate nominals in the past tense?
Feature_Description:
Clauses that express proper inclusion, equation, attribution, location, existence, and possession may be collectively referred to as predicate nominals. Here, we are only interested in what could, in a more restricted sense, be referred to as *predicate nominals* and *predicate adjectives*. They involve the structure *NP copula NP* or *NP copula AdjP*, where *copula* usually designates a verb, but it may also be an affix, or a particle that joins two nominal elements ([Payne 1997](sources.bib?label=Payne+1997#cldf:payne_1997):115; [Pustet 2003](sources.bib?label=Pustet+2003#cldf:pustet_2003):5). This question asks whether a copula is commonly used in the past tense, as in examples (1) and (2) (for clauses with present time reference, see UT045). (1) Livonian
>*Se **vȯļ** tä’m tidār mīez*
>this be.PST.3SG 3SG.GEN daughter.GEN husband
>‘This was his daughter’s husband’ (2) Nganasan
>*betі͡aŋɨči ńaagəəɁ **isʲüəɁ***
>betі͡aŋɨ-či ńaagəə-Ɂ i-sʲüə-Ɂ
>broad.whitefish-PL.3SGPOSS good-PL be-PST-3PL
>'The briad whitefish were good'
([Wagner-Nagy 2018](sources.bib?label=Wagner-Nagy+2018#cldf:wagner-nagy_grammar_2018):350) ## Coding The value is '1' if a copula is commonly used in clauses with past time reference. When answering this question only affirmative past tense forms were considered.

Name: Can locative constructions commonly occur without a copula?
Feature_Description:
Locative predicates are a type of nonverbal predicate used to convey location ([Dryer 2007](sources.bib?label=Dryer+2007#cldf:Dryer_2007)). A locative predicate involves a locative adverbial phrase that is usually a noun (or a prepositional) phrase or a clause with locational meaning. The aim of this question is to determine whether locative predicates can commonly occur without a copula, as in example (1). (1) South Saami
>*Manne ålkone*
>I out
>'I am outside’ By comparison, in Finnish it is not common to omit a copula in such cases: (2) Finnish
>*Minä olen ulkona*
>minä ole-n ulkona
>I be-1SG out
>‘I am outside’ ## Coding The value is '1' if locative adverbial phrases are commonly attested without a copula (usually a 'be'-verb), as in South Saami (1). If locative predicates typically require a copula, as in Finnish (2), the value is '0'.

Name: Is the same coordinating conjunction commonly used for NPs, VPs, and clauses?
Feature_Description:
Coordination is used to mean joining syntactic constructions of the same type into larger units. This may be done by simply placing two units next to each other (juxtaposition) or by applying a linker or a coordinator ([Velupillai 2012](sources.bib?label=Velupillai+2012#cldf:Velupillai2012):318). This question asks whether it is common to use the same coordinator for conjunctive coordination (i.e., 'and'-coordination) in the case of NPs, VPs, and clauses, as in example (1a-c). (1) Hungarian
>a. *Anna **és** Péter* ‘Anna and Peter’
>b. *Péter olvas **és** tanul* ‘Peter is reading and studying’
>c. *Péter olvas **és** Anna tanul* ‘Peter is reading and Anna is studying' ## Coding The value is '1' if the same coordinating conjunction is commonly used for NPs, VPs, and clauses. The answer is '0' if a different strategy (e.g., juxtaposition) is more typical in at least one of the cases.

Name: Does existential negation have a special negator?
Feature_Description:
The negative strategies used for standard negation (i.e., negation of declarative verbal main clauses) often differ from stategies used in existentials, imperatives, and nonverbal clauses ([Miestamo 2007](sources.bib?label=Miestamo+2007#cldf:Miestamo2007)). This question investigates whether existentials require a special negative marker not found with standard negation. For instance, the Hungarian *nincs* occurs in existentials (1a), whereas *nem* is used for standard negation (1b). (1) Hungarian
>a. ***Nincs** igazság a Földön*
>nincs igazság a Föld-ön
>NEG justice ART world-ADE
>‘There is no justice in the world’
>([Rounds 2001](sources.bib?label=Rounds+2001#cldf:rounds_hungarian_2001): 271) >b. *jövök : **nem** jövök*
>jövö-k : nem jövö-k
>come-1SG : NEG come-1SG
>'I'm coming : I'm not coming' ## Coding The value is '1' if existential and standard negation are expressed by different negative markers (as in Hungarian).

Name: Can standard negation be asymmetric in the present tense?
Feature_Description:
Standard negation stands for the negation of declerative verbal main clauses. A distinction can be made between symmetric and asymmetric negation. Whereas in the case of symmetric negation the affirmative and negative clauses only differ by the negative marker, asymmetric negation differs in other ways too, e.g., negation may affect the finiteness of verbal elements, marking of grammatical categories, reality status of events, etc. ([Miestamo 2013](sources.bib?label=Miestamo+2013#cldf:wals-114)). Both systems can co-occur in a language. For example, in Livonian, plural forms only differ in terms of the negative marker (= symmetric negation), while singular forms additionally show differences in person marking (= asymmetric negation) ([Metslang et al. 2015](sources.bib?label=Metslang+et+al.+2015#cldf:metslang_et_al_2015):435), cf. examples (1a) and (1b). The aim of this question is to examine the occurrence of asymmetric standard negation in the present tense. (1) Livonian
>a. *ma **lugūb** vs. ma **äb lu’g***
>ma lugū-b vs. ma äb lu’g
>1SG read-1SG vs. 1SG NEG.PRS.1SG read.CNG
>‘I read’ vs. ‘I don’t read’ >b. *mēg **lu’ggõm** vs. mēg **äb lu’ggõm***
>mēg lu’gg-õm vs. mēg äb lu’gg-õm
>1PL read-1PL vs. 1PL NEG.1PL read-1PL
>‘we read’ vs. ‘we don’t read’ ## Coding The answer is '1', if in the present tense, standard negation is asymmetric in at least some part of the negative paradigm (as is the case with Livonian). The answer is '0' if, in the present tense, standard negation can only be symmetric.

Name: Can non-finite dependent clauses use the standard negation construction?
Feature_Description:
This question investigates whether the standard negation construction can also be used in non-finite dependent clauses. For instance, in Hungarian, the negative marker *nem* can be used in both contexts (see 1a and 1b). (1) Hungarian
>a. ***Nem** látva a kutyát, megbotlott*
>nem lát-va a kutyá-t, megbotl-ott
>NEG see-PTCP DEF dog-ACC trip.over-PST.3SG
>'As he did not see the dog, he tripped over [it]'
>b. ***Nem** látta meg a kutyát és megbotlott*
>nem lát-ta meg a kutyá-t és megbotl-ott
>NEG see-PST.3SG PRV DEF dog and trip.over-PST.3SG
>'He did not see the dog and tripped over [it]'
By comparison, the Estonian particle *ei*, which appears in the standard negation construction is not compatible with non-finite dependent clauses and *mitte* has to be used instead (see UT052). ## Coding The value is '1' if the standard negation construction can also be used in non-finite dependent clauses (as in Hungarian). The answer is '0' if non-finite dependent clauses make use of a different negative marker or an entirely different negation construction is employed. ## Note This question was provided by Ksenia Shagal & Matti Miestamo.

Name: Is there a special negator that combines with non-finite forms?
Feature_Description:
A language may contain a particle, suffix or some other negative marker that only appears with non-finite forms, i.e. it cannot negate finite verbs. In Beserman Udmurt, the present participle in *-š’* and non-past participle in *-no* are negated by a regular adjectival negative marker *-tem* (“a derivative suffix with caritive meaning” according to [Edygarova 2015](sources.bib?label=Edygarova+2015#cldf:edygarova_2015): 278), while the past participle in *-m* is negated with a specialized participial negative marker *-te*. Both of these markers count as special non-finite negators. In Estonian, there is a special negator *mitte*, which is used to negate non-finite forms (1a), while *ei* is used in standard negation construction (cf. 1b). (1) Estonian
>a. *Küsimus-t **mitte** mõistes ta **ei** vasta-nud*
>question-PRT not understand.CVB s/he NEG answer-ACT.PST.PTCP
>'Having not understood the question, s/he did not answer'
>b. *Ta **ei** mõist-nud küsimus-t, seega ta **ei** vastanud*
>3SG NEG understand-ACT.PST.PTCP question-PRT so 3SG NEG answer-ACT.PST.PTCP
>‘S/he did not understand the question, so s/he did not reply’
## Coding The value is '1' if there is a special negative marker that is compatible with non-finite forms. Markers of non-finite forms that are inherently negative (i.e. cannot be analysed as a combination of two separate markers) were not considered when aswering this question (cf. UT053). ## Note This question was provided by Ksenia Shagal & Matti Miestamo.

Name: Are there special negative non-finite forms?
Feature_Description:
Special negative non-finite forms are forms that can be considered as inherently negative. In such cases, the affixes cannot be analysed in a straightforward way as the respective affirmative marker + a negative marker. In Northern Khanty, for instance, there are two affirmative participles, the non-past participle in *-ti* and past participle in *-m*, and they are both negated by the form in *-li* (1). (1) Northern Khanty
>a. *pe:jal-ti xo:s-**li** ńa:wre:m il su:wil-ǝ-ti pit-ǝ-s*
>swim-INF can-PTCP.NEG child down drown-EP-INF start-EP-PST.3SG
>‘A child who could not swim started drowning’
>b. *jo:nt-**li** je:rnas śuŋ-na xu:j-ǝ-l*
>sew-PTCP.NEG dress corner-LOC lie-EP-NONPST.3SG
>‘A dress which someone did not finish sewing lies in the corner’
([Nikolaeva 1999](sources.bib?label=Nikolaeva+1999#cldf:nikolaeva_semantics_1999):34; cited through [Shagal 2018](sources.bib?label=Shagal+2018#cldf:shagal_participial_2018)) The Finnish negative participle in *-maton* is diachronically *-ma* + nominal negator *-ton* (cf. *asunto* ‘apartment’ + *-ton = asunnoton* ‘the one without an apartment’), but it also has many idiosyncratic features, for instance, it can negate all participles (e.g., *koskaan kuolematon rakkaus* ‘the love that never dies’), including the agentive participle in *-ma*. Therefore, it can be regarded as a special negative non-finite form. ## Coding The value is '1' if a language has inherently negative non-finite forms as described above. If no such forms can be found the answer is '0'. ## Note This question was provided by Ksenia Shagal & Matti Miestamo.

Name: Is there an impersonal passive construction?
Feature_Description:
A common voice distinction is between active vs. passive. Certain kinds of oppositions, however, can be better described as a distinction between personal vs. impersonal voice (see also [Blevins 2003](sources.bib?label=Blevins+2003#cldf:BLEVINS_2003)). Although the communicative function of passive and impersonal may be similar, the corresponding constructions show differences in their underlying morphosyntax. Whereas passivization is a valency-reducing operation, as a result of impersonalization, the subject is deleted but no constituent is promoted to the subject position (argument realization is constrained), e.g., in (1a) the object retains its case form (cf. kuula-n muusika-t [listen-1SG music-PRT] ‘I listen to the music’). Impersonalization may involve intransitive verbs such as ‘to be’, ‘to live’ (1b), etc. (1) Estonian
>a. ***Kuulatakse** muusikat*
>kuula-takse muusika-t
>listen-IPS.PRS music-PRT
>‘One listens to music’
>b. *Siin **elatakse** hästi*
>siin ela-takse hästi
>here live-IPS.PRS well
>'People live well here’ (lit. ‘It is lived well here’) ## Coding The value is '1' if impersonalization is possible, i.e., the argument structure of the active clause may be retained and/or impersonalization can also involve intransitive verbs.

Name: Are there non-finite forms specializing in the relativization of particular core participants, e.g., subjects (S/A), direct objects (P), absolutive participants (S/P)?
Feature_Description:
Participles can be described on the basis of their capacity to relativize participants of a situation. Such participants include S (the single participant of an intransitive clause or the intransitive subject), A (the agent-like participant of a transitive clause), P (the patient-like participant of a transitive clause) and various oblique participants (locatives, instrumentals, etc.) (see [Shagal 2017](sources.bib?label=Shagal+2017#cldf:shagal_2017)). This question asks whether there are participles that can relativize only one specific participant (i.e., only S/A, P or S/P) and be inherently oriented towards that specific participant. Such participles can be found in Finnish, where active participles are S/A-oriented, i.e., they relativize subjects (1a), whereas passive participles are P-oriented, i.e. they relativize objects (1b) ([Shagal 2018](sources.bib?label=Shagal+2018#cldf:shagal_participial_2018):65). There is no one participle that can do both (e.g., *luettu* cannot be used for 1a, nor can *lukeva* be used for 1b). (1) Finnish
>a. ***lukeva** poika*
>luke-va poika
>read-ACT.PST.PTCP boy
>‘a reading boy’ (< *poika, joka lukee (kirjaa)* 'a boy who is reading (a book)'; *poika* 'boy' = S/A; thus, the active present participle relativizes subjects)
>b. ***luettu** kirja*
>lue-ttu kirja
>read-PASS.PST.PTCP book
>‘a book that has been read’ (< *lukea kirjaa* 'to read a book'; *kirja* 'book' = P; thus, the passive past participle relativizes P-arguments) In some languages, the relevant forms may be referred to as nominalizations or converbs. ## Coding The value is '1' if there is at least one such non-finite form, which specializes in relativizin one of these arguments. If one participle can relativize S/A, P, and S/P, the answer is '0'.

Name: Are there non-finite forms that can relativize all core participants (S/A/P) and possibly also some non-core participants?
Feature_Description:
A participle may be used to relativize only one specific participant of a situation (i.e., only S/A, P or S/P; see UT055), but there are also participles that can relativize all core participants and possibly also some non-core participants, such as locatives and instrumentals ([Shagal 2017](sources.bib?label=Shagal+2017#cldf:shagal_2017): 1-3). For example, Hill Mari *-šašlə̑k* (1) can be used in a wide variety of contexts including for relativizing locatives ([Shagal 2018](sources.bib?label=Shagal+2018#cldf:shagal_participial_2018)). (1) Hill Mari
>*[Vas’a(-n) ə̈lə̈-šäšlə̑k] pört-ə̈m mə̈n’ stroj-en-äm*
>Vasya(-GEN) live-PTCP.DEB house-ACC 1SG[NOM] build-PRF-1SG
>‘I built the house in which Vasya will live’
([Shagal 2018](sources.bib?label=Shagal+2018#cldf:shagal_participial_2018):57) In some languages, the relevant forms may be referred to as nominalizations or converbs. ## Coding The value is '1' if a language contains at least one participle, which can relativize all core participants and possibly also some non-core participants.

Name: Are there non-finite forms that can relativize at least some non-core participants but cannot relativize all core participants?
Feature_Description:
In addition to the types covered in questions UT055 and UT056, it is possible to find participles that can relativize some non-core participants (e.g., instrumentals, locatives) but not all core participants. For instance, in Beserman Udmurt there is a participle ending in *-n*, which can be used to relativize instrumentals and direct objects (P) (1a and 1b, respectively) but not subjects (S/A) ([Shagal 2018](sources.bib?label=Shagal+2018#cldf:shagal_participial_2018):74). (1) Beserman Udmurt
>a. *valez **š’už’jan** sǝ̑nanze Vaš’a ǝ̑stiz*
>[val-ez š’už’ja-n] sǝ̑na-n-z-e Vaš’a ǝ̑st-i-z*
>horse-3 clean-PTCP comb-PTCP-3-ACC Vasya lose-PST-3
>‘Vasya lost the comb with which he used to clean the horse’
>b. *mǝ̑nam nǝ̑le jarate mǝ̑niš’tem **lǝ̑ǯ’on** kn’igame*
> *mǝ̑nam nǝ̑l-e jarat-e mǝ̑niš’tem [lǝ̑ǯ’-on] kn’iga-m-e*
>1SG.GEN1 daughter-1SG love-PRS.3SG 1SG.GEN2 read-PTCP book-1-ACC
>‘My daughter loves the book that I read to her’
(Brykina and Aralova 2012:511, cited through [Shagal 2018](sources.bib?label=Shagal+2018#cldf:shagal_participial_2018):74) In some languages, the relevant forms may be referred to as nominalizations or converbs. ## Coding The value is '1' if there is at least one participle that can relativize some non-core participants but cannot relativize all or some core participants.

Name: Is there a specialized negative participial form, which cannot be analyzed as a specific participial marker + negative marker?
Feature_Description:
The aim of this question is to find out whether there is a negative participle, which cannot be analyzed as a combination of a specific participial marker and a negative marker. Such participle can be found in Northern Khanty (see *-li* in example 1), which is also neutral as regards temporal and aspectual characteristics associated with two affirmative participles in the language (see [Shagal 2018](sources.bib?label=Shagal+2018#cldf:shagal_participial_2018):78). (1) Northern Khanty
>*[pe:jal-ti xo:s-**li**] ńa:wre:m il su:wil-ǝ-ti pit-ǝ-s*
>swim-INF can-PTCP.NEG child down drown-EP-INF start-EP-PST.3SG
>‘A child who could not swim started drowning’
([Nikolaeva 1999](sources.bib?label=Nikolaeva+1999#cldf:nikolaeva_semantics_1999): 34, cited through [Shagal 2018](sources.bib?label=Shagal+2018#cldf:shagal_participial_2018):78) To compare, in Erzya participial clauses are negated using a negative marker (example 2) and thus for Erzya the value is '0'. (2) Erzya
>*[a / avol’ lovn-ic’a] t’ejt’er’*
>NEG / NEG read-PTCP.PRS girl
>‘a girl who does not read’
## Coding The value is '1' if a language contains a specialized negative participle (as in Northern Khanty). If there is no such participle and participial clauses are negated simply using a negative marker (as in Erzya), the value is '0'.

Name: Is there a future participle?
Feature_Description:
To be regarded as a future participle the coresponding participle needs to denote an action that will/must/should be carried out or occur in the future. For example, according to [Krasnova et al. ](sources.bib?label=Krasnova+et+al.+#cldf:krasnova_reading_2017)([2017](sources.bib?label=2017#cldf:krasnova_reading_2017)), the Hill Mari marker *-šašlə̑k* is primarily used for future situations, but it sometimes also appears in debitive contexts. (1) Hill Mari
>***качшашлык** киндӹ*
>kɑt͡ʃ-ʃɑʃlək kində̈
>eat-PTCP.FUT bread
>'bread that will/should be eaten'
>([Krasnova et al. 2017](sources.bib?label=Krasnova+et+al.+2017#cldf:krasnova_reading_2017):162) ## Coding The value is '1' if there is a participle that gives rise to a future interpretation (but possibly also appears in some modal contexts). The value is '0' if there is no participle that can receive a future interpretation. Examples where a future interpretation arises only from context or is associated with a particular construction were not counted.

Name: Are there participles that can encode their subject with possessive suffixes?
Feature_Description:
In Finnish, the possessive marking may be used to encode the subject of a situation (1-2) ([Shagal 2018](sources.bib?label=Shagal+2018#cldf:shagal_participial_2018); [Hakulinen et al. 2004](sources.bib?label=Hakulinen+et+al.+2004#cldf:hakulinen_iso_2004): § 527). By comparison, in finite clauses, the subject is expressed with the nominative. The Finnish agentive participle and passive participle (2) are employed only for direct object relativization. (1) Finnish
>***tekemäni** työ*
>teke-mä-ni työ
>do-AG.PTCP-POSS.1SG work
>‘Work that I did’
(2) Finnish
>***Kuvattavakseni** tilhi ei antaudu*
>kuva-tta-va-kse-ni tilhi ei antaudu >photograph-PASS-PRS.PTCP-TRNSL-POSS.1SG waxwing NEG surrender
>’The waxwing won't let me photograph it’
>([Hakulinen et al. 2004](sources.bib?label=Hakulinen+et+al.+2004#cldf:hakulinen_iso_2004): § 533)
## Coding The value is '1' if a possessive suffix encoding the subject can attach to a participle. The value is '0' if there are no possessive suffixes in a language or they occur in some other position (e.g., on the head noun).

Name: Are there participles that must agree with the nouns they modify in case and number in all contexts?
Feature_Description:
This question asks whether there are participles that obligatorily agree with the nouns they modify in case and number. This is possible, for instance, in Finnish (1). (1) Finnish
>*Puhuin eilen **lähetetyistä** kirjeistä*
>puhuin eilen lähete-ty-i-stä kirje-i-stä
>speak-PST-1SG yesterday send-PTCP.PST-PL-ELA letter-PL-ELA
>‘I was speaking about the letters that were sent yesterday’
Here, "all contexts" is used to mean contexts where one would also expect agreement otherwise, e.g., with adnominal modifiers (cf. example 2). (2) Finnish
>***uusista** kirjeistä*
>uus-i-sta kirje-i-stä
>new-PL-ELA letter-PL-ELA
>'(about) new letters' ## Coding The value is '1' if there is at least one participle, which obligatorily agrees in case and number with the noun it modifies in all contexts.

Name: Are there non-finite forms that can be used both as participles and action nominalizations?
Feature_Description:
Non-finite forms can be found in various functions. This question asks whether it is possible that one and the same form appears both as a participle and as an action nominalization (see 1a and 1b, respectively). (1) Komi Zyrian (Pechora dialect)
>a. *[mama-li̮s’ **vur-e̮m**] de̮re̮m me kos’al-i*
>mother-GEN2 sew-PTCP shirt 1SG tear-PST
>‘I’ve torn the shirt mother sewed’
>b. *[mama-le̮n de̮re̮m **vur-e̮m**] meni̮m kažitс’-e̮*
>mother-GEN shirt sew-NMLZ 1SG.DAT like-PRS.3
>‘I like the way mother has sewn the shirt’
(Serdobolskaya and Paperno 2006:1, cited through [Shagal 2018](sources.bib?label=Shagal+2018#cldf:shagal_participial_2018):59) For an overlap between the form functioning as a participle and as an agentive nominalization, see UT064. ## Coding The value is '1' if a non-finite form can be used both as a participle and as an action nominalization without any additional morphology.

Name: Are there non-finite forms that can be used both as participles and agentive nominalizations?
Feature_Description:
This question asks whether it is possible for one and the same form to function both as a participle and an agentive nominalization (examples 1a-b). (1) Livonian
>a. ***a'iliji***
>a'ili-ji
>run-AG.NMLZ
>'a runner'
>b. ***a'iliji** i'bbi*
>a'ili-ji i'bbi
>run-AG.NMLZ horse.NOM
>‘a running horse’ (lit. ‘a runner horse’)
For the overlap between a participle and an action nominalization, see UT063. ## Coding The value is '1' if a non-finite form can be used both as a participle and an agentive nominalization without any additional morphology.

Name: Are there non-finite forms that can be used both as participles and converbs?
Feature_Description:
Non-finite forms can be used in various functions, including as converbs. A converb can be defined as "a nonfinite verb form whose main function is to mark adverbial subordination"; whereas converbs could be seen as verbal adverbs, participles could be regarded as verbal adjectives ([Haspelmath 1995](sources.bib?label=Haspelmath+1995#cldf:haspelmath_1995):3). This question asks whether one and the same form can be used in both functions. (Converbs may be subsumed under different labels, e.g., in Udmurt they are typically called gerunds.) (1) Udmurt
>a. ***валамон** уж*
>vala-mon už
>understand-PTCP work
>'comprehensible work'
>b. ***валамон** вераськиз*
>vɑlɑ-mon verɑ-ɕki-z
>understand-GER speak-PST-3SG
>'S/he spoke comprehensibly'
(Nikolay Kuznetsov and Svetlana Edygarova, p.c.) ## Coding The value is '1' if there is a non-finite form that can function both as a participle and a converb without any additional morphological material.

Name: Is OV the neutral order in affirmative imperative clauses?
Feature_Description:
The aim of this question is to determine whether OV is the neutral order in affirmative imperative clauses, as in Udmurt (1). (1) Udmurt
>*чай ю!
>t͡ɕɑj ju*
>tea[ACC] drink.IMP.2SG
>'Drink tea!'
>(Nikolay Kuznetsov, p.c.) ## Coding The value is '1' only if OV represents the neutral order. If OV is possible but not neutral, the value is '0'.

Name: Is it possible to place an object between the negative marker and the main verb in imperative constructions?
Feature_Description:
The aim of this question is to determine whether an object can appear between the negative marker and the main verb in negative imperatives, as in example (1). (1) Estonian
>*Ära **mind** unusta!*
>NEG.IMP 1SG.PRT forget.CNG
>‘Don’t forget me’ By comparison, in Udmurt, nothing can intervene between the negative auxiliary and the main verb (example 2). Thus, Udmurt is coded as ‘0’. (2) Udmurt
>*En das’a **s’ion**!*
>NEG prepare.IMP:2SG meal.NOM
>‘Don’t prepare a meal!’
>([Edygarova et al. 2017](sources.bib?label=Edygarova+et+al.+2017#cldf:edygarova_et_al_2017))
## Coding The value is '1', if an object can appear between the negative marker and the main verb. If an object cannot intervene, the value is ‘0’.

Name: Is there diminutive (hypocoristic) marking on verbs?
Feature_Description:
The aim of this question is to determine whether there is any diminutive marking on the verb that can indicate attitudinal information (affection, intimacy, etc.) of the speaker towards the referent. For example, the diminutive suffixes *-kwe* and *-risj* (example 1) in North Mansi can express the speaker’s positive emotional attitude towards the described state of affairs (*-risj* may also convey a sense of pity) ([Bakró-Nagy et al. 2022](sources.bib?label=Bakr%C3%B3-Nagy+et+al.+2022#cldf:bakro-nagy_north_mansi_2022):549). The terms *precaritive* and *hypocoristic* have also been used. (1) North Mansi
>*ɑkweːkw, wɑːɣ-tɑːl pɑt-əm-risj-ən*
>grandmother strength-CAR get-MIR.PST-DIM-2SG
>‘Grandmother, poor dear, you are tired!’
>([Bakró-Nagy et al. 2022](sources.bib?label=Bakr%C3%B3-Nagy+et+al.+2022#cldf:bakro-nagy_north_mansi_2022):549) Instances in which the diminutive marking on the verb yields a quantitative or temporal reading ('a little bit, for a while'), see example 2, do not suffice for ‘1’ for to this question. (2) Komi Zyrian
>*Ме эськö сёйышті*
>Me eɕkɘ ɕoj-ɨʃt-i
>1SG COND eat-DIM-PST.1SG
>’I would eat a little bit’
>(Nikolay Kuznetsov, p.c.) ## Coding The answer is '1' if there is a diminutive marker on the verb that expresses attitudinal information. If diminutives cannot be used on the verb, or they appear in the reading ‘a little bit, for a while’, the answer is ‘0’.

Name: Can modifiers of action nominalizations in agent and patient roles both be encoded like possessors?
Feature_Description:
The aim of the question is to determine whether modifiers of action nominalizations can be encoded like possessors in agent and patient roles. The Estonian example (1a) contains an agent, while (1b) contains a patient. There are also examples where two interpretations are possible, e.g., in (1c) *poisi* can be understood as an agent or patient (depending on the context). In all examples (1a-c), genitive encoding appears, which is also used for possessors (cf. 1d). (1) Estonian
>a. ***minu** ütlemine* (S)
>minu ütle-mine
>1SG.GEN say-NMLZ
>'my saying' >b. ***saapa** viskamine* (P)
>saapa viska-mine
>boot.GEN throw-NMLZ
>'boot throwing' >c. ***poisi** pesemine* (S/P)
>poisi pese-mine
>boy.GEN wash-NMLZ
>'washing of the boy (i.e. done by the boy)'/ 'washing the boy' >d. ***poisi** tuba*
>boy.GEN room
>‘boy's room' ## Coding The value is '1' if possessors can be encoded in the same way as modifiers of action nominalizations in agent and patient roles. To be encoded as '1', it is at least necessary to have examples like (1a) and (1b). The value is '1' also if they are all zero-marked.

Name: Can the modifier of action nominalizations in the patient role be encoded like a direct object?
Feature_Description:
The aim of the question is to determine whether the same encoding can be used for the modifier of an action nominalization in the patient role and a direct object. In Estonian, genitive encoding is possible in both situations (see 1a and 1b). (1) Estonian
>a. ***luuletuse** kirjutamine*
>luuletuse kirjuta-mine
>poem.GEN write-NMZ
>'a poem writing' >b. *kirjutan **luuletuse***
>kirjuta-n luuletuse
>write-1SG poem.GEN
>'I'll write a poem' ## Coding The value is '1' if the same case marking can be used in both situations (as in the Estonian examples).

Name: Do core adjectives agree with the subject noun in number in the predicative position?
Feature_Description:
Most adjectives have two positions, i.e. they can be used attributively (e.g., *tall boy*) and predicatively (e.g., *the boy is tall*). When used predicatively, the adjective may be accompanied by a copula but not necessarily; it is also possible for the morphological marking of adjectives to differ in these two contexts ([Hofherr 2010](sources.bib?label=Hofherr+2010#cldf:CabredoHofherr2010):10; [Schachter and Shopen 2007](sources.bib?label=Schachter+and+Shopen+2007#cldf:Schachter_Shopen_2007):13). This question examines whether it is common for adjectives occurring in the predicative position to agree with the subject noun in number, as in Hungarian (1) and Estonian (2). (Following Grambank, *core adjectives* are defined semantically as property concepts such as value, shape, age, dimension.) (1) Hungarian
>*a **fiúk magasak***
>a fiú-k magas-ak
>DEF.ART boy-PL tall-PL
>‘The boys are tall’ (2) Estonian
>***poisid** on **pikad***
>poisi-d on pika-d
>boy-PL be.3 tall-PL
>'The boys are tall' ## Coding The answer is '1' if it is common for core adjectives used predicatively to agree with the subject noun in number. The answer is '0' if there is no agreement.

Name: Is there a bound superlative degree marker on property words?
Feature_Description:
This question asks whether there is a bound marker of superlative degree on property words, as in the Finnish example (1). Property words roughly correspond to adjectives. (1) Finnish
>*kaunis : **kaunein***
>kaunis : kaune-in
>beautiful : beautiful-SPRL
>‘beautiful' : 'the most beautiful’ To compare, in Erzya, the corresponding constructions are formed by using *veśe* 'all' in the ablative case followed by an adjective in the nominative case ([Aasmäe 2012](sources.bib?label=Aasm%C3%A4e+2012#cldf:aasmae_2012)). Thus, Erzya is coded as '0'. ## Coding The answer is '1' if a language has a morphological marking available to express the highest quality/degree (as in Finnish). The answer is '0' if there is no bound marker available (as in Erzya).

Name: Can adpositions have person/number marking?
Feature_Description:
Adpositions supplement the case system by providing extra ways of expressing temporal, spatial, and other types of relationship, e.g., in the Hungarian example (1a), *mellett* conveys a spatial relationship. Adpositions also share several characteristics with cases, for instance, they may be used as stems to which personal suffixes are attached. This question examines weather person/number marking is possible on words that are also used as adpositions (see 1b). (1) Hungarian
>a. *a múzeum mellett*
> DEF.ART museum next
>'next to the museum'
>b. ***mellettem** : **melletted***, etc.
>mellette-m : mellette-d
>next-POSS.1SG : next-POSS.2SG
> 'next to me, you, etc.'
## Coding The answer is '1' if person/number markers can be attached to what also occur s adpositional stems. If this is not possible, the answer is '0'.

Name: Are there adpositions that show a tripartite system?
Feature_Description:
Adpositions with the same stem may encode a three-way distinction, i.e., express a distinction between goal (also called *destination of movement*), location, and source of movement (for case marking, see also UT091 and UT092). As conflation in systems of spatial adpositions is relatively common (especially of location and destination marking; see, e.g., [Creissels 2012](sources.bib?label=Creissels+2012#cldf:creissels_2012)), this question only targets instances that reveal a tripartite system without there being syncretism, as in examples (1a-c). (1) Estonian
>a. *Jalutan maja **juurde***
>jaluta-n maja juurde
>walk-1SG house.GEN near.ILL
>‘I walk near the house’
>b. *Olen maja **juures***
>ole-n maja juures
>be-1SG house.GEN near.INE
>‘I am near the house’
>*c. Tulen maja **juurest***
>tule-n maja juurest
>come-1SG house.GEN near.ELA
>‘I come from near the house’
Locations that are more commonly marked for direction are AT < IN, ON < UNDER < BEHIND < FRONT ([Zwarts 2010](sources.bib?label=Zwarts+2010#cldf:Zwarts_2010):984). ## Coding The answer is '1' if such a three-way distinction as in (1a-c) is possible at least with some adpositions expressing AT, IN/ON, UNDER, BEHIND, FRONT. If no tripartite system can be attested, the answer is '0'.

Name: Is there case compounding?
Feature_Description:
Case compounding is usually understood as attachment of multiple inflectional case markers to a noun stem that make up a phonological word. The phenomenon is also known as Suffixaufnahme (see [Plank 1995](sources.bib?label=Plank+1995#cldf:plank_double_1995)). Several types of case compounding can be attested. The Udmurt example (1) is an instance of a simple headless adnominal, which additionally includes a case for the elided head. In Komi, it is possible that several spatial cases are combined to form a complex spatial case ([Arkhangelskiy and Usacheva 2018](sources.bib?label=Arkhangelskiy+and+Usacheva+2018#cldf:arkhangelskiy_case_2018):115). (1) Udmurt
>*gurtlenezlen*
>gurt-len-ez-len
>village-GEN-POSS.3SG-GEN
>‘of the one that belongs to the vilage’
>(Arkhangelskiy and Usacheva 2019:112) ## Coding The value is '1' if at least two case markers can be attached to the noun stem. The prerequisite is that the examples contain proper case endings.

Name: Are there morphological cases only used with pronouns?
Feature_Description:
The same way that case inventories can vary in languages (from two to dozens), different words or word classes may display different numbers of cases, e.g., nouns may distinguish more cases than adjectives ([Bickel and Nichols 2007](sources.bib?label=Bickel+and+Nichols+2007#cldf:Bickel_Nichols_2007):210). The aim of this question is to determine whether pronouns stand out by displaying morphological cases not found with other word classes (e.g., with nouns or adjectives). For example, the accusative case in Finnish is only used with pronouns, cf. the case marking of *sinä* 'you' and *kirja* 'book' (1). (1) Finnish
>*Minä haluan **sinut** / kirjan*
>minä halua-n sinu-t / kirja-n
>1SG want-1SG you-ACC / book-GEN
>‘I want you / the book’ ## Coding The answer is '1' if pronouns display at least one morphological case not found with other word classes. If there are no such cases, the answer is '0'.

Name: Does the numeral modification of a noun have an effect on its case selection?
Feature_Description:
This question asks whether there are morphosyntactic differences between phrases containing cardinal numerals. For example, in Finnish, the cardinal numeral 'one' requires the nominative case, whereas cardinal numerals bigger than 'one' take the partitive case (1). (1) Finnish
>*yksi ystävä : viisi ystävää*
>one.NOM.SG friend.NOM.SG : five.NOM.SG friend.PRT.SG
